title: Mapping Reversal Pathways and Interaction Fields in Artificial Spin Ice
authors: Brindaban Ojha, Matías P. Grassi, Vassilios Kapaklis
abstract: In artificial spin ice (ASI), magnetic interactions between nanomagnets determine both the stable states and the switching pathways under an applied field. Here, first-order reversal curve (FORC) measurements are used to map how these interactions govern magnetization reversal in square arrays as the element shape and spacing are varied. The FORC diagrams show that some geometries reverse more uniformly, whereas others exhibit broader, more asymmetric responses, indicating stronger interaction effects and more complex reversal pathways. Combined FORC analysis and micromagnetic simulations also capture subtle changes in internal magnetization textures during switching, linking local behavior within individual elements to collective behavior across the array. These results establish FORC as a practical tool for mapping and engineering interaction landscapes, with direct relevance to reconfigurable magnetic reservoirs and neuromorphic functionality.
summary: This paper investigates how the geometry and spacing of nanomagnets in artificial spin ice control magnetic reversal and interaction strengths. By using FORC measurements and micromagnetic simulations, the authors show that changing the aspect ratio can trigger complex internal magnetization textures, while decreasing the lattice pitch enhances dipolar coupling. These findings provide a way to engineer interaction landscapes, which is crucial for developing new types of magnetic-based neuromorphic computing and reservoir computing architectures.
main_problem: Understanding how nanomagnet geometry and lattice pitch govern magnetization reversal pathways and the strength of dipolar interactions in artificial spin ice (ASI).
main_result: The study demonstrates that reducing aspect ratio induces complex C-type domain textures (boomerang FORC features), while reducing lattice pitch strengthens dipolar interactions, leading to a broad distribution of local bias fields.
method: First-order reversal curve (FORC) measurements using the Magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) combined with MuMax3 micromagnetic simulations.
model_or_system: Square artificial spin ice (ASI) arrays consisting of lithographically defined ferromagnetic Permalloy (Ni80Fe20) nano-islands on a Ta/AlOx multilayer stack.
key_observables: FORC diagrams (contour plots), magnetization traces (minor loops), interaction field (Hu), and coercive field (Hc), and magnetic textures (S-type, C-type, and mixed states).
important_parameters: Nanomagnet length, width, aspect ratio, lattice pitch, and demagnetization energy density.
figures_summary: FORC diagrams showing boomerang features for increased width (S2) and vertical elongation for reduced pitch (S3); magnetization reversal sequences through intermediate C and S states; and illustrations of C- and S-state magnetic domain textures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of mapping interaction landscapes, describes the experimental and simulation setup, analyzes three different geometric configurations (S1, S2, S3) to isolate intra-island and inter-island effects, and concludes with implications for neuromorphic computing.
why_interesting: The study explores how local interactions and geometric constraints drive collective behavior and complex switching landscapes, which is conceptually related to the study of glassy dynamics and reconfigurable networks in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Scanning electron microscopy images of square artificial spin ice arrays patterned by electron-beam lithography (EBL). The nanomagnets have lateral dimensions of l1 = 430 nm, w1 = 100 nm, and a1 = 700 nm for S1 (a); l1 = 430 nm, w1 = 140 nm, and a1 = 700 nm for S2 (b); and l1 = 430 nm, w1 = 100 nm, and a1 = 530 nm for S3 (c). During FORC measurements, the field was applied along the [1,1] direction of the lattice. The scale bar is 400 nm.
Figure 2: (a) Processed family of FORCs acquired for S1. (b) Corresponding FORC diagram of S1.
Figure 3: (a) Representative FORCs of S2. (b) Corresponding FORC diagram in the Hu–Hc coordinate system. Inset: zoomed-in view of the central peak with a boomerang-shaped feature.

Figure 4: (a) A family of FORCs for S3 with the applied field along the [1,1] direction of the lattice. (b) Corresponding FORC diagram plot- ted in the Hc–Hu coordinate system. Inset: zoomed-in view of the central peak highlighting its vertical spreading.
Figure 5: Simulated FORC distributions of Case I (S1) (a), Case II (S2) (b), and Case III (S3) (c). Inset: square array of 36 nanomagnets used in the simulations.

Figure 6: Evolution of magnetic texture in nanomagnets as a function of applied field for Case I (S1), Case II (S2), and Case III (S3). The top, middle, and bottom rows correspond to the simulated counterparts of S1, S2, and S3, respectively. The images show the field-driven reversal sequence from the negative reversal state toward positive saturation, highlighting the distinct intermediate configurations that emerge in each case, including S-domain states, mixed S + C states, and C-state-mediated reversal. The comparison illustrates how changes in geometry and interaction strength modify the reversal pathway and the complexity of the internal magnetic textures.
